original_abstract: Transition metal oxides typically undergo dynamic surface reconstruction under oxygen evolution reaction (OER) conditions to achieve their active states, a process that varies significantly according to the electrolyte pH. While this pH-dependent catalytic activity is a widely observed phenomenon, the underlying mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this study, we utilize operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy to monitor the evolution of cobalt (Co) oxidation states across various pH-directed (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interfaces. By integrating these observations with in situ electrochemical analysis, we establish critical correlations between Co redox dynamics, flat band potentials, and oxidation state transitions to elucidate the pH dependency of OER activity. Our results demonstrate that alkaline environments lower the flat band potential, facilitating Co redox transformations at lower potentials and promoting beneficial surface reconstruction. Conversely, neutral and acidic environments trigger an anodic shift in these transformations, thereby increasing the catalytic overpotential. In neutral media, this higher overpotential is further driven by limited Co atom polarizability and sluggish oxidation state transitions. These findings underscore that the kinetics and thermodynamics of interfacial Co oxidation state changes are the primary determinants of pH-dependent oxygen evolution efficiency.
humanized_abstract: Transition metal oxides undergo changes in their surface structure that can occur dynamically in order to access the active structure for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In general, the pH dependent nature of the catalytic activity of transition metal oxides remains unclear. This study applies operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy to study the Co oxidation state variations that differ significantly in dependence on the pH directed (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interface formed in the solution. Supplementing these data with in situ electrochemical measurements, we elucidate essential links between the Co redox activity, the flat band potentials and the Co oxidation state changes that allow insight into the OER activity as a function of pH. We show that the catalytic performance of a transition metal oxide for OER can be regulated in significant detail by adjusting the pH of the electrolyte, based on facilitating favorable surface structures under specific conditions as demonstrated here for Co-based catalysts under alkaline solution conditions; alkaline solutions reduce the position of the flat band potential, enabling the reduction and oxidation of Co centers at lower potentials, together with accompanying favorable surface structures. Under neutral and acidic solution conditions the oxidation is located at higher potentials than the corresponding reduction potentials, resulting in an increased overpotential that under neutral pH solution conditions is additionally determined by large Co atom polarizability and slow oxidation state changes occurring under low pH.
